text: The laparoscopic view shows the gallbladder within the peritoneal cavity, completely covered by the omentum.
The surgeon attempts to separate the omentum from the head of the gallbladder, gradually creating space to manipulate and push back the gallbladder.

The surgeon encounters varying adhesion strengths near the gallbladder, particularly on the lateral right side of the patient. The omentum is inflamed and appears reddish, with stronger adhesions at the fundus. The surgeon uses a tooth forceps to gradually separate these adhesions, aiming to hold and push back the head of the gallbladder.

The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation to control bleeding while manipulating the gallbladder to expose its attachment to the cystic duct. The gallbladder is pushed back to reach the base, where it connects to the cystic duct and common hepatic duct.

The surgeon uses a tooth forceps to grasp the gallbladder fundus and push it back, causing some of the omentum to fall away while other parts remain adhered. The inflamed tissue shows dilated vessels due to increased blood flow.

The surgeon continues dissection in the region of Calot's triangle, separating the thickened visceral peritoneum from the gallbladder. The cystic artery becomes visible as the peritoneum peels off, revealing its translucent appearance.

The surgeon uses a tooth grasper to hold the gallbladder fundus, while the gallbladder appears merged with the common hepatic duct, common bile duct, and portal vein below. Adhesions are present, and the surgeon employs a Maryland curve dissector to carefully dissect and find a cleavage plane, staying close to the gallbladder to separate it from the ducts and secure them.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct leading to the common bile duct and attempts to find a dissection plane between the gallbladder and the common hepatic duct. The gallbladder bed is exposed above the liver's hepatic fossa, with visible liver tissue lacking peritoneal coverage. The surgeon carefully dissects close to the gallbladder to avoid bleeding from potential venous sinuses in the liver. The cystic artery is visible, adherent to the gallbladder, and is being separated with some force, causing minor bleeding.

The surgeon carefully isolates the cystic duct at the base of the gallbladder, below the infundibulum, using gentle pressure and a curved Maryland dissector. The gallbladder is pulled upwards and towards the left side to expose the space between the gallbladder and surrounding tissues, protecting the major biliary ducts below.

The surgeon carefully peels the gallbladder away from the underlying structures, including the major biliary ducts, portal vein, and hepatic artery, which are encased in fibrous tissue. Using gentle traction and counter-traction, a plane is developed posterior to the gallbladder, ensuring no harm is caused to the vital structures during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects the thickened tissue near the gallbladder fundus, identifying and delineating the cystic duct and cystic artery. Clips are applied to the cystic duct, ensuring it is safely ligated without damaging nearby vital structures. The anatomy is clearly visible, with the gallbladder fossa well dissected.

The surgeon applies clips to the cystic duct and cystic artery, ensuring secure placement by using two clips proximally on each structure. After clipping, the surgeon cuts between the clips using a monopolar cautery hook. The gallbladder, which contains a single stone, is thickened near the fundus, suggesting chronic inflammation. The gallbladder is submitted for histopathological examination to rule out malignancy.

The surgeon observes chronic inflammation and adhesions around the gallbladder, with visible redness and thickened tissue. The omentum is adherent, indicating possible contained perforations. The gallbladder fundus remains slightly attached to the liver, and the surgeon continues dissection while maintaining hemostasis at the gallbladder bed.
